title: The small business boom - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: US new-business formation hit a two-decade record of 5.7 million applications last year and keeps climbing, a boom that started with pandemic-era layoffs and remote work and is now being supercharged by generative AI lowering the cost and difficulty of starting a company.

insights.claim: Americans filed 5.7 million new business applications last year, the most in the two decades the Census Bureau has tracked the data, with new applications continuing to climb through the first half of this year.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A record, still-accelerating rate of new business formation is a leading indicator of entrepreneurial dynamism and future small-business-driven employment and investment demand.
insights.locator: Americans filed 5.7 million applications last year

insights.claim: The current US startup boom was jump-started by pandemic-era mass layoffs and the shift to remote work, and has since been supercharged by the rise of artificial intelligence, ending a decades-long slump in start-up activity.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Identifies AI as a second, distinct tailwind on top of the pandemic shock, suggesting the startup boom has structural legs beyond a one-off pandemic bounce.

insights.claim: A study by economists at the University of British Columbia and the Stockholm School of Economics found generative AI is spurring entrepreneurial activity in the United States, both by giving rise to new AI-centered ventures and by making it cheaper to start any enterprise.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Academic confirmation that generative AI is reducing the fixed cost of starting a business -- a structural driver investors can expect to persist, favoring tools/platforms that serve solo and small-team founders.

insights.claim: Jan Bena, a University of British Columbia associate professor and co-author of the entrepreneurship study, attributes the surge in business entry to AI tools' broad, general-purpose capability.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Suggests the entrepreneurship boost from AI is broad-based across sectors rather than confined to AI-native companies.
insights.quote: A.I. tools can do very many different things very well, that's the reason why you see so much entry.

insights.claim: According to a Gusto report, nearly 60% of founders on its payroll and benefits platform who started businesses last year said they used AI, and half of those said AI made starting the business cheaper and faster.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A concrete adoption metric quantifying AI's role in new-business formation, useful for sizing the AI-driven share of the startup boom.
insights.locator: nearly 60 percent of founders on its platform
